ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried kai lan with mushroom; mapped to the closest parent class 'stir-fried vegetable dish' (FOODON_03311737). Exact dish-level code does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for stir-fried kai lan with mushroom. Mapped to the closest defensible category for cooked leafy vegetables (A00LC). Mushroom component is a secondary ingredient not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept exists for this specific stir-fried vegetable dish. SNOMED CT is not applicable for this food record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried kai lan with mushroom weighs approximately 300 g, which is a typical single-person serving as sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Stir-fried Vegetables (Kai Lan / Chinese Broccoli, cooked with sauce)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for stir-fried kai lan with mushroom as a combined dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried Chinese broccoli (kai lan) with oyster sauce, supplemented with USDA FoodData Central data for mushroom contribution (USDA SR Legacy: Mushrooms, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — FDC ID 169251). The input record values (energy 93.33 kcal, protein 3.33 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 6.67 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB-range estimates for this dish type and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 400 mg/100 g is consistent with oyster-sauce-seasoned stir-fried vegetables in the HPB database.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database: Broccoli and leafy green vegetables (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried kai lan with mushroom. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked Chinese broccoli / leafy green vegetables (GI ~30–35, low category). Mushrooms have negligible glycaemic impact (GI <15). Oyster sauce contributes a small sugar load but does not materially raise the dish GI above the low threshold. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (32) × carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 6.67 g/100 g = 20.01 g) / 100 = 6.4 g. This is a low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg per 100 g — at the upper boundary of the moderate-concern threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: A standard 300 g plate delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, which represents 52–60% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; patients with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce, or choose a smaller portion, to keep daily sodium within target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and modest carbohydrate content (6.67 g per 100 g) with dietary fibre supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried kai lan with mushroom has a low GI and a low glycaemic load per serving (~6.4), making it a suitable vegetable side dish for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, patients should monitor sauce volume as oyster sauce contributes both sugar and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (400 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (1.0 g/100 g) and trans fat is absent, which are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and free of trans fat, which supports cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from oyster and soy sauces poses a risk for blood pressure elevation; patients with CVD should request sauce reduction and limit portion size to manage cumulative sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Kai lan provides sulforaphane (anti-inflammatory) and mushrooms provide beta-glucans (immune-modulating); saturated fat is low and trans fat is absent
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for people with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory vegetable components; patients should request reduced oyster and soy sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Kai Lan with Mushroom (炒芥兰香菇) is a popular Singaporean hawker and food-court vegetable dish featuring kai lan (Chinese broccoli, Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) and mushrooms — typically shiitake or button — wok-tossed over high heat with garlic, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The dish delivers crisp-tender kai lan stalks and leaves with an earthy, savoury mushroom flavour and a glossy, umami-rich sauce. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 93 kcal per 100 g, with 3.3 g protein, 6.0 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), and 6.7 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg per 100 g due to the oyster and soy sauce seasoning, making sodium management the primary clinical consideration. The dish has a low glycaemic index (~32) and is rich in vitamin C (13.3 mg/100 g), calcium (33.3 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (1.67 g/100 g). Kai lan is a cruciferous vegetable containing sulforaphane, a compound with documented anti-inflammatory properties. Mushrooms contribute beta-glucans that support immune modulation. The dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and modest carbohydrate load, but caution is warranted for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients because of the high sodium content. A standard serving is one plate (approximately 300 g), delivering around 280 kcal and 1200 mg sodium. The dish is commonly served as a vegetable side at Singaporean hawker centres, Thai restaurants, and Chinese food stalls across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for stir-fried kai lan with mushroom as a combined dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried Chinese broccoli with oyster sauce and USDA FoodData Central mushroom data. The input record values are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked leafy green vegetables. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this stir-fried dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this combined dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this preparation; estimated potassium ~260 mg/100 g from component analysis is noted in health context but not entered as a confirmed value. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this dish type as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible — no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation; vegetable oil and oyster sauce do not contribute trans fats. The input record listed primary_cuisine as Thai; this has been retained as a secondary cuisine influence (Thai-style preparation) while Singapore is recorded as the country of origin, consistent with the GrabFood listing context. The dish is sold under menu code 603D. Halal status is marked true as the dish uses vegetable oil and plant-based ingredients; however, oyster sauce may contain shellfish derivatives — consumers with shellfish allergies should verify. Kosher status is false as oyster sauce is not kosher.
